JB
PART I:
INTRODUCTION TO
THE STRAIGHT LINE SYSTEM
PART 3

-- 1 of 8 --

SHAPING YOUR INNER WORLD
ou could say The Straight Line System is a redeemer of lost souls. Those
who are lost in the past, lost in their life’s mistakes, lost to disempowering
beliefs, can all be redeemed. And renewed. It’s already happened tens of thousands
of times. It begins with …
A view toward the future
The truth is, you can’t earn millions until you’ve convinced yourself you’re capable
of earning millions and are worthy of earning millions. That requires seeing
yourself, not as you are now, but as you will be in the future.
The sheer enjoyment of visualizing your future state of success is what changes
your current outlook. Play your success movie in your head enough times and
you’ll soon adopt habits associated with successful people.
You’ll get out of bed earlier, have more energy throughout the day and remain
focused on what’s really important. You’ll be able to better form your vision and
articulate it in a way that will attract like-minded people. Your confidence level
will soar and that will cause others around you to have more confidence in you.
© 2015 by Jordan Belfort. All rights reserved. 	www.JordanBelfort.com
Y

-- 2 of 8 --

JORDAN BELFORT
You’ll dress smarter, act smarter and all in all, you’ll be a much happier person.
You’ll know where you’re headed, how you’ll get there and what reward is waiting
for you upon arrival. Most important, you’ll control the content of your success
movie because you are the scriptwriter, director and producer.
Ask just about anyone who’s won a Gold Medal or a Super Bowl ring, scaled an
imposing mountain or ran a marathon, they all saw their victory time and time
again, before it ever happened in real life. It’s called future pacing, a technique
used in state management – the management of your emotional state.
Future pacing makes state management effective in the long term as you’ve just
discovered. State management can also be effective in the present moment. Like
when you’re making a cold call, you’re entering into negotiations, or you’re facing
an important decision and you can’t afford to have a single negative emotion get
in your way.
These negative emotions, from anger to impatience to hopelessness and insecurity
hold us firmly in a disempowered state. That’s why people who are emotionally
down tend to stay down. And why as adults we hold on to the limiting beliefs of
our childhood.
You can stop spinning your wheels, gain emotional traction and move forward by
learning to trigger these four key states: certainty, clarity, confidence, and courage
(the four Cs). They’re the difference between hoping you’ll be in the right
emotional state when the time comes, versus knowing you’ll be in the right
emotional state.
The decision is all yours
At any given moment you can choose to feel a certain way, even if it’s contrary to
what should be the “norm.” So you can walk into a sales meeting brimming with
certainty, clarity, confidence, and courage despite having just gotten a speeding
ticket because you were running late because the alarm didn't go off because there
© 2015 by Jordan Belfort. All rights reserved. 	www.JordanBelfort.com

-- 3 of 8 --

JORDAN BELFORT
was a power failure last night. And your tummy is rumbling because you missed
breakfast and oh yeah, there’s a tension headache coming on. Is that enough crap
to deal with at 9 am?
If you were to focus on everything that’s wrong (and hey, it won’t take much effort
at all) you can pretty much bet your presentation will go down in flames. But
what if you could shove all that crap into a dark room and slam the door shut?
What you’d see then is all the good things in your life, everything that had been,
for the moment, overshadowed by all that crap.
The truth is, for the moment, you can create a positive emotional state that will
get you through tough times. There’s nothing mystical about this because when
you get right down to it, your brain is a gray matter computer. And like any
computer, it can be programmed to act a certain way when given a certain
command. In fact, since early childhood, your brain has been programmed and
reprogrammed, and often not in ways that are good for you. But that’s another
issue for another time.
Right now we’re looking to avoid those disempowering beliefs that come so
readily to mind. You’ll first need to focus on positive images – everything that’s
right in your life. Then you’ll need to move your body in a positive manner. This
psychological/physiological duo is stunningly potent.
But … all this is easier said than done. That’s because we really have to work at
getting from a disempowering state to an empowering state. It doesn’t happen
with the snap of the fingers. Then again, it can happen that way and that’s exactly
where we’re heading with all this.
Reprogramming for fun and profit
Remember Pavlov’s dog? The Russian physiologist’s landmark experiment went
something like this: present a dog with food while ringing a bell. Dog sees food,
hears bell and salivates at the sight of food. Yum! Rinse and repeat a few times.
Then throw a curveball. Ring bell, but offer no food. Dog still salivates. Lesson
© 2015 by Jordan Belfort. All rights reserved. 	www.JordanBelfort.com

-- 4 of 8 --

JORDAN BELFORT
learned: Dog associates sound of bell with the yum of food. Initially, salivation
was triggered by the food. But in the end, just the sound of the bell was enough to
get the dog to salivate.
You can apply Pavlov’s findings to your own life. A simple example would be a
feel-good song from your past. Every time you hear it, you just feel good. It brings
back … memories! We’re going a step further though – into the world of neuro
linguistic programming (NLP). You won’t need a Ph.D for this, but you will need
to catch some new lingo.
The bell in Pavlov’s experiment is called an anchor in NLP. When you ring the
bell you fire off the anchor. And when you associate the bell with the food you set
the anchor.
Throw me an anchor!
Now, remember the four key emotional states from earlier in this study? They are
certainty, clarity, confidence, and courage? Well, they are your “food.” When
you’re stressed, under the gun or in any way feeling in a disempowered state at a
time when you really need to be on your toes, you’re going to need a healthy
serving of some or all of the four Cs. And we’re going to do this by taking a
moment in your past when you were at the height of certainty, clarity, confidence,
and courage and we’re going to set an anchor to that memory. It’ll take some work
to get there, but here’s the good part: next time you need to get out of a funk you
can virtually flick a switch and change your emotional state from negative to
positive.
This process of anchoring requires five steps. They are:
1. 	Choose a state
2. 	Choose your focus
3. 	Change your physiology
4. 	Intensify your state
5. 	Set the anchor
© 2015 by Jordan Belfort. All rights reserved. 	www.JordanBelfort.com

-- 5 of 8 --

JORDAN BELFORT
Choose your state is pretty straightforward. You have four to choose from:
certainty, clarity, confidence, and courage.
To choose your focus, go back in time and search for a moment when you were at
the height of one of those four Cs. So feeling pretty certain about something or
being fairly confident won’t cut the mustard. You’re looking for what amounts to
your crowning achievement. An event so monumental in your life you can
remember every little detail to this day.
So what are those details? What do you see, and hear, and smell? What are you
feeling as the tension, danger, suspense, mystery builds. What do you feel the
moment you realize the victory is yours? Hold on to that feeling and savor it for as
long as you can.
Change your physiology from what it is now to what it is as you relive the object
of your focus. Get specific. How are you standing, sitting, running, jumping? Can
you feel your heart beat? How would you describe your pulse and breathing rate?
What are you doing with your hands? What about facial expressions: deadpan,
wide-eyed, radiant, playful, carefree?
Intensify your state by incorporating all five senses (NLP: modalities) Don’t just
see, but see in 3-D technicolor with super HD, hear in 24-channel surround
sound; touch, taste and smell as if you are right there in the moment.
Set the anchor by associating your state with any modality stimulus. Remember,
modalities are your five senses. A bell worked for Pavlov’s dog. The same may
work for you. Or any other sound-making device. Maybe a clap of your hands or
the snap of your fingers. What about taste and smell? Maybe an old-fashioned
sour ball. Or a pungent aroma.
Do choose an anchor that makes you sit up and take notice. So nothing too mild
nor anything you’re already too familiar with. Make it unique, something to be
used only for a special occasion. It should be easily portable, accessible at a
moment’s notice and ideally, subtle enough that only you know when you set it
off. So blowing a tuba or cracking open an aged limburger definitely are out of
the question.
© 2015 by Jordan Belfort. All rights reserved. 	www.JordanBelfort.com

-- 6 of 8 --

JORDAN BELFORT
Let’s go out in style!
One more thought, and this one’s the game changer. Some people have a tough
time synching their state and their anchor. The memory isn’t vivid enough or
notable enough or whatever. No problem. Here’s what you do ...
You go ahead and choose your anchor and then you wait until you close your next
sale. And right when you’re at the height of any or all of your four Cs, and you
couldn’t possibly feel any more exuberant, you set that anchor. Bam! Your gray
matter computer just recorded all the action and emotion of a winning close.
Now, with a single command, you can flood all five of your senses with with the
very same experience, at will. So powerful!
A quick review
1. 	You can program your mind to “forget” past mistakes and replace
disempowering beliefs with empowering beliefs; negative emotions with
positive emotions.
2. 	One way to do this is through future pacing where you see yourself in the
future in a positive light.
3. 	Visualizing your future state of success changes your current outlook. You
begin to adopt habits associated with successful people.
4. 	Future pacing is one aspect of state management – the management of
your emotional state.
5. 	State management is comprised of four key states: certainty, clarity,
confidence, and courage (the four Cs).
6. 	By vividly recalling the details of a past favorable event and associating the
event with a particular sensory stimulus, you can replace negative feelings
with positive feelings when the sensory stimulus is activated.
© 2015 by Jordan Belfort. All rights reserved. 	www.JordanBelfort.com

-- 7 of 8 --

JORDAN BELFORT
7. 	In neuro linguistic programming (NLP), the sensory stimulus is called an
anchor. Activating the stimulus is called firing off the anchor. Associating
the favorable event with the stimulus is called setting the anchor.
8. 	Anchoring requires five steps:
- 	Choose a state (certainty, clarity, confidence, and courage)
- 	Choose your focus (the details of a past favorable event)
- 	Change your physiology (act as if you’re experiencing that past favorable
event)
- 	Intensify your state (by incorporating all five senses to recall every little
detail)
- 	Set the anchor (associate the favorable event with the stimulus)
9. 	In lieu of setting an anchor for a past event and even better choice may be
to wait for a winning event, such as closing a sale, and setting your anchor
while you’re consumed in victory.
© 2015 by Jordan Belfort. All rights reserved. 	www.JordanBelfort.com

-- 8 of 8 --
